## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Next: gar-namespace-path-tokens (₢BBAA2) [rough]

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Wire-format value rename plus centralization. Mechanical sweep across
  all GAR path producers and consumers; no semantic change. The
  mechanism — central attribute reference feeding many consumers — is
  the load-bearing piece.
  
  ## Goals
  
  Replace the three GAR top-level subdirectory literals with short,
  unique, anchored path tokens, held centrally:
  
  - `enshrines/`   → `rbi_es/`
  - `hallmarks/`   → `rbi_hm/`
  - `reliquaries/` → `rbi_rq/`
  
  **Spec corpus** — three AsciiDoc attribute references in RBS0's
  mapping section, each mapping a descriptive key to its literal value:
  
  ```
  :gar_enshrines_namespace:   rbi_es
  :gar_hallmarks_namespace:   rbi_hm
  :gar_reliquaries_namespace: rbi_rq
  ```
  
  These are plain attribute substitutions, not linked-term quoins —
  no definition site, no anchor; the value is the concept. RBS0
  includes every other spec, so attributes are visible corpus-wide.
  
  Spec prose: replace literal `enshrines/` with `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/`
  (and similarly for the other two) at every wire-format use site.
  Conceptual prose ("files held in the enshrines") stays bare English —
  no quoin needed, no attribute reference needed.
  
  **Code** — three named constants in the appropriate kindle/tinder
  module, descriptive names with the short values:
  
  ```
  RBI_ENSHRINES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_es"
  RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"
  RBI_RELIQUARIES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_rq"
  ```
  
  All call sites composing GAR locators reference the constants rather
  than the bare literal strings.
  
  ## Wrap criterion
  
  - Three AsciiDoc attribute references emplaced in RBS0's mapping
    section.
  - All wire-format occurrences in spec prose use `{attr}` substitution;
    conceptual prose left as bare English.
  - Three named constants emplaced in the appropriate kindle/tinder
    module; all bash/rust call sites composing GAR locators reference
    the constants.
  - Locator parses (the inline `${anchor%:*}` / `${anchor##*:}` pattern
    used at three sites in BBAAz) operate identically on the new tokens.
  - Existing populated value migrated: the lone `enshrines/...` ANCHOR
    field repo-wide in `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/rbrv.env`.
  - Listing-block (`----`) audit: any spec code samples carrying these
    literals get `subs="attributes"` or a deliberate-literal exception
    documented.
  - Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Code-identifier prefix mintings (`rbie_`, `rbih_`, `rbir_` were
    considered and rejected — wire-format only).
  - Section concept quoins (e.g. `gar_enshrines_section`) — bare English
    carries the conceptual reference; only wire values are centralized.
  - New tabtarget colophons; the existing `rbw-i*` family is unchanged.
  - Migration of any abandoned-vessel registry artifacts under the old
    paths — fresh-build assumption.
  
  ## References
  
  - BBAAz locator-shape refactor — locator parsing pattern; this pace
    swaps the values flowing through that pattern.
  - RBS0 mapping section — existing precedent for attribute references
    visible across all included specs.
  - Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — kindle/tinder convention.

  - Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Code-identifier prefix mintings (`rbie_`, `rbih_`, `rbir_` were
    considered and rejected — wire-format only).
  - Section concept quoins (e.g. `gar_enshrines_section`) — bare English
    carries the conceptual reference; only wire values are centralized.
  - New tabtarget colophons; the existing `rbw-i*` family is unchanged.
  - Migration of any abandoned-vessel registry artifacts under the old
    paths — fresh-build assumption.
  
  ## References
  
  - BBAAz locator-shape refactor — locator parsing pattern; this pace
    swaps the values flowing through that pattern.
  - RBS0 mapping section — existing precedent for attribute references
    visible across all included specs.
  - Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — kindle/tinder convention.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
6	
7	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
8	
9	## Single operator prerequisite chain
10	
11	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
12	
13	## Entry contract — load-bearing
14	
15	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
16	
17	- Working tree clean
18	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
19	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
20	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
21	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
22	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
23	
24	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
25	
26	## Failure mode contract
27	
28	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
29	
30	## Release-branch execution contract
31	
32	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
33	
34	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
35	
36	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
37	
38	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
39	
40	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
41	
42	## Tier layering
43	
44	Three tiers, escalating cost:
45	
46	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
47	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
48	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
49	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
50	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
51	
52	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
53	
54	## Gauntlet test suite — design
55	
56	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
57	
58	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
59	
60	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
61	
62	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
63	
64	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
65	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
66	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
67	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
68	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
69	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
70	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
71	
72	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
73	
74	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
75	
76	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
77	
78	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
79	
80	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
81	
82	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
83	
84	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
85	
86	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
87	
88	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
89	
90	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
91	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
92	
93	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
94	
95	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
96	
97	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
98	
99	## Suite-level fail-fast
100	
101	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
102	
103	## No-teardown decision
104	
105	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
106	
107	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
108	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
109	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
110	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
111	
112	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).
113	
114	## Operator scope
115	
116	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
117	
118	## Coupling to ₣A_
119	
120	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
121	
122	## Locked design decisions
123	
124	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
125	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
126	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
127	
128	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
129	
130	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
131	
132	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
133	
134	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
135	
136	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
137	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
138	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
139	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
140	
141	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
142	
143	Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.
144	
145	## References
146	
147	- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
148	- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
149	- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
150	- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
151	- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)
152	
153	# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA2
154	
155	## Character
156	
157	Wire-format value rename plus centralization. Mechanical sweep across
158	all GAR path producers and consumers; no semantic change. The
159	mechanism — central attribute reference feeding many consumers — is
160	the load-bearing piece.
161	
162	## Goals
163	
164	Replace the three GAR top-level subdirectory literals with short,
165	unique, anchored path tokens, held centrally:
166	
167	- `enshrines/`   → `rbi_es/`
168	- `hallmarks/`   → `rbi_hm/`
169	- `reliquaries/` → `rbi_rq/`
170	
171	**Spec corpus** — three AsciiDoc attribute references in RBS0's
172	mapping section, each mapping a descriptive key to its literal value:
173	
174	```
175	:gar_enshrines_namespace:   rbi_es
176	:gar_hallmarks_namespace:   rbi_hm
177	:gar_reliquaries_namespace: rbi_rq
178	```
179	
180	These are plain attribute substitutions, not linked-term quoins —
181	no definition site, no anchor; the value is the concept. RBS0
182	includes every other spec, so attributes are visible corpus-wide.
183	
184	Spec prose: replace literal `enshrines/` with `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/`
185	(and similarly for the other two) at every wire-format use site.
186	Conceptual prose ("files held in the enshrines") stays bare English —
187	no quoin needed, no attribute reference needed.
188	
189	**Code** — three named constants in the appropriate kindle/tinder
190	module, descriptive names with the short values:
191	
192	```
193	RBI_ENSHRINES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_es"
194	RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"
195	RBI_RELIQUARIES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_rq"
196	```
197	
198	All call sites composing GAR locators reference the constants rather
199	than the bare literal strings.
200	
201	## Wrap criterion
202	
203	- Three AsciiDoc attribute references emplaced in RBS0's mapping
204	  section.
205	- All wire-format occurrences in spec prose use `{attr}` substitution;
206	  conceptual prose left as bare English.
207	- Three named constants emplaced in the appropriate kindle/tinder
208	  module; all bash/rust call sites composing GAR locators reference
209	  the constants.
210	- Locator parses (the inline `${anchor%:*}` / `${anchor##*:}` pattern
211	  used at three sites in BBAAz) operate identically on the new tokens.

214	- Listing-block (`----`) audit: any spec code samples carrying these

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony is BBAAI work (RELEASE.md).

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition (BBAAH)

# jjezs_pace ₢BBAA2

## Character

Wire-format value rename plus centralization. Mechanical sweep across
all GAR path producers and consumers; no semantic change. The
mechanism — central attribute reference feeding many consumers — is
the load-bearing piece.

## Goals

Replace the three GAR top-level subdirectory literals with short,
unique, anchored path tokens, held centrally:

- `enshrines/`   → `rbi_es/`
- `hallmarks/`   → `rbi_hm/`
- `reliquaries/` → `rbi_rq/`

**Spec corpus** — three AsciiDoc attribute references in RBS0's
mapping section, each mapping a descriptive key to its literal value:

```
:gar_enshrines_namespace:   rbi_es
:gar_hallmarks_namespace:   rbi_hm
:gar_reliquaries_namespace: rbi_rq
```

These are plain attribute substitutions, not linked-term quoins —
no definition site, no anchor; the value is the concept. RBS0
includes every other spec, so attributes are visible corpus-wide.

Spec prose: replace literal `enshrines/` with `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/`
(and similarly for the other two) at every wire-format use site.
Conceptual prose ("files held in the enshrines") stays bare English —
no quoin needed, no attribute reference needed.

**Code** — three named constants in the appropriate kindle/tinder
module, descriptive names with the short values:

```
RBI_ENSHRINES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_es"
RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"
RBI_RELIQUARIES_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_rq"
```

All call sites composing GAR locators reference the constants rather
than the bare literal strings.

## Wrap criterion

- Three AsciiDoc attribute references emplaced in RBS0's mapping
  section.
- All wire-format occurrences in spec prose use `{attr}` substitution;
  conceptual prose left as bare English.
- Three named constants emplaced in the appropriate kindle/tinder
  module; all bash/rust call sites composing GAR locators reference
  the constants.
- Locator parses (the inline `${anchor%:*}` / `${anchor##*:}` pattern
  used at three sites in BBAAz) operate identically on the new tokens.
- Existing populated value migrated: the lone `enshrines/...` ANCHOR
  field repo-wide in `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/rbrv.env`.
- Listing-block (`----`) audit: any spec code samples carrying these
  literals get `subs="attributes"` or a deliberate-literal exception
  documented.
- Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence cases unchanged.

## Out of scope

- Code-identifier prefix mintings (`rbie_`, `rbih_`, `rbir_` were
  considered and rejected — wire-format only).
- Section concept quoins (e.g. `gar_enshrines_section`) — bare English
  carries the conceptual reference; only wire values are centralized.
- New tabtarget colophons; the existing `rbw-i*` family is unchanged.
- Migration of any abandoned-vessel registry artifacts under the old
  paths — fresh-build assumption.

## References

- BBAAz locator-shape refactor — locator parsing pattern; this pace
  swaps the values flowing through that pattern.
- RBS0 mapping section — existing precedent for attribute references
  visible across all included specs.
- Tools/buk/buk-claude-context.md — kindle/tinder convention.

content.text:
Mounted ₣BB. Here's the orientation.

## Heat: rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification (₣BB) — 30/45 racing

**Paddock shape:** Constructing `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine, a third release-tier that refuses to run unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The gauntlet test suite walks a state ladder (enrollment-validation → pristine-lifecycle → canonical-establish → canonical-onboarding-sequence → regime tests → four-mode → per-crucible) to catch silent first-build assumptions. Locked decisions: depot identity collapsed to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; cult-verb naming (SA→roster, image→audit); build/run split; calibrant fixture family for ₣BC.

**Just landed (BBAAz):** Redefined `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` from bare identifier to domain-blind locator `enshrines/<anchor>:<anchor>` matching the wrest/jettison `package-path:tag` pattern. Establishes the parse pattern (`${anchor%:*}` / `${anchor##*:}`) that this pace's tokens will flow through.

## Next: ₢BBAA2 gar-namespace-path-tokens [rough]

**What:** Mechanical wire-format rename + centralization. Three GAR top-level subdirectory literals get short anchored tokens, held centrally:
- `enshrines/` → `rbi_es/`
- `hallmarks/` → `rbi_hm/`
- `reliquaries/` → `rbi_rq/`

**Surfaces:**
1. Three AsciiDoc attribute references in RBS0 mapping section (plain substitutions, not quoins)
2. Three named bash constants in kindle/tinder module
3. Spec prose: literal → `{attr}` at wire-format use sites only; conceptual prose stays bare English
4. All bash/rust call sites composing GAR locators reference the constants
5. Migrate the lone populated value: `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/rbrv.env`
6. Listing-block audit: `subs="attributes"` where needed
7. Fast suite green; canonical onboarding-sequence unchanged

**Silks check:** "gar-namespace-path-tokens" fits — accurate and concise. No rename needed.

## Approach

- **Discovery first**: locate every `enshrines/`, `hallmarks/`, `reliquaries/` literal across spec corpus and code; classify each as wire-format (needs substitution) vs conceptual prose (leave bare).
- **Spec-side**: emplace 3 attribute references in RBS0; sweep `.adoc` use-sites; audit listing blocks for `subs="attributes"`.
- **Code-side**: identify the right kindle/tinder home for the constants (likely `rbgc_Constants.sh` or an image-domain module — will check); add the 3 constants; sweep bash + rust call sites.
- **Migrate** the populated `rbrv.env` value.
- **Verify**: fast suite + canonical-onboarding-sequence cases.

## Execution strategy

**Sequential, opus.** Mechanical in shape but the wire-format-vs-conceptual classification needs judgment per occurrence — premature parallelism risks miscategorizing. The locator parse must continue to work identically post-swap, so I'll verify the parse pattern at the three BBAAz sites against new tokens. Single thread keeps the spec/code symmetry coherent.

Ready to proceed?

46:**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
50:External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
54:- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
55:- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
63:zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
79:**What can call kindle**:
81:- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
90:function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
99:| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
100:| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
106:Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
120:| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
121:| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
123:| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
127:**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
129:**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
131:**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
133:**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
210:  z«prefix»_kindle
211:  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
244:      zheavy_kindle
251:  z«prefix»_kindle
280:# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
287:z«prefix»_kindle() {
288:  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
301:  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
304:  # Kindle constants (internal)
313:  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
317:  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
348:**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
351:# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
353:  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
356:# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
357:z«prefix»_kindle() {
359:  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
363:**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
366:# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
367:«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
369:# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
373:# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
374:«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
376:# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
377:«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
379:# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
380:«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
382:# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
383:«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
393:"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
534:**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
539:# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
607:#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
609:Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
612:z«prefix»_kindle() {
613:  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
615:  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
623:  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
626:  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
630:If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
632:**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
634:#### Mutable Kindle State

49	  readonly RBGL_RELIQUARIES_ROOT="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_RELIQUARIES}"

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle GAR Layout - Implementation
#
# Kindle constants for GAR categorical-namespace path construction.
# Callers interpolate the three root constants with hallmark/date/anchor and
# basename literals at the call site — no subshell, no function call overhead.
#
# Shape:
#   "${z_gar_base}/${RBGL_HALLMARKS_ROOT}/<hallmark>/<basename>:<hallmark>"
#   "${z_gar_base}/${RBGL_RELIQUARIES_ROOT}/<date>/<tool>:<date>"
#   "${z_gar_base}/${RBGL_ENSHRINES_ROOT}/<anchor>:<anchor>"

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBGL_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgl multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBGL_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbgl_*)

zrbgl_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBGL_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgl already kindled"

  # Category constants come from rbgc; cloud prefix comes from rbrr.
  zrbgc_sentinel
  test -n "${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX+x}" \
    || buc_die "RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX must be defined before rbgl kindle"

  # Root segments — cloud-prefix + category. Callers append '/<id>/<basename>:<tag>'.
  readonly RBGL_HALLMARKS_ROOT="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS}"
  readonly RBGL_RELIQUARIES_ROOT="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_RELIQUARIES}"
  readonly RBGL_ENSHRINES_ROOT="${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}${RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_ENSHRINES}"

  readonly ZRBGL_KINDLED=1
}

zrbgl_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBGL_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbgl not kindled - call zrbgl_kindle first"
}

# eof

135:  readonly RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_ENSHRINES="enshrines"

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle GCP Constants - Implementation (no printf required)

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
# (Module state remains ZRBGC_* per BCG; external constants use RBGC_*)
test -z "${ZRBGC_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgc multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBGC_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbgc_*)

zrbgc_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBGC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbgc already kindled"

  # Global Resource Naming (Google Cloud global namespace)
  # These resources compete in globally-unique namespaces across all of GCP
  # Pattern: {prefix}-{type}-{name}-{timestamp} where timestamp is YYMMDDHHMMSS
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_PREFIX="rbwg"
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TYPE_PAYOR="p"
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TYPE_DEPOT="d"
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TYPE_BUCKET="b"
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TIMESTAMP_FORMAT="+%y%m%d%H%M%S"
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TIMESTAMP_LEN=12
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_TIMESTAMP_REGEX="[0-9]{${RBGC_GLOBAL_TIMESTAMP_LEN}}"

  # Global resource validation patterns
  # Payor:  rbwg-p-YYMMDDHHMMSS  (timestamp survives — payor is installation-scoped, not depot-scoped)
  readonly RBGC_GLOBAL_PAYOR_REGEX="^${RBGC_GLOBAL_PREFIX}-${RBGC_GLOBAL_TYPE_PAYOR}-${RBGC_GLOBAL_TIMESTAMP_REGEX}$"

  # Basic Configuration
  readonly RBGC_ADMIN_ROLE="rbw-admin"
  readonly RBGC_PAYOR_ROLE="rbw-payor"
  readonly RBGC_PAYOR_APP_NAME="Recipe Bottle Payor"

  # Timeouts
  readonly RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC=90
  readonly RBGC_EVENTUAL_CONSISTENCY_SEC=3
  readonly RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX=7
  readonly RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC=10

  # JWT-bearer consumer-side retry — every RBRA consumer absorbs the post-write
  # race where Google's OAuth backend has not yet accepted a freshly-minted SA
  # key, surfaced as `invalid_grant` + `Invalid JWT Signature` on token mint.
  # 90s budget locked by ₣BB pristine-tier contract; cadence below.
  readonly RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_BUDGET_SEC=90
  readonly RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC=2
  readonly RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_MAX_DELAY_SEC=15

  # URL Roots & Well-known Endpoints
  readonly RBGC_OAUTH_TOKEN_URL="https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_IAM="https://iam.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM="https://cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_SERVICEUSAGE="https://serviceusage.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY="https://artifactregistry.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_CLOUDBUILD="https://cloudbuild.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_CLOUDBILLING="https://cloudbilling.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_STORAGE="https://storage.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_API_ROOT_SECRETMANAGER="https://secretmanager.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_CONSOLE_URL="https://console.cloud.google.com/"
  readonly RBGC_SIGNUP_URL="https://cloud.google.com/free"

  # OAuth Scopes
  readonly RBGC_SCOPE_CLOUD_PLATFORM="https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform"

  # Service Usage Service Identifiers
  readonly RBGC_SERVICE_IAM="iam.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_SERVICE_CRM="cloudresourcemanager.googleapis.com"
  readonly RBGC_SERVICE_ARTIFACTREGISTRY="artifactregistry.googleapis.com"

  # Email/Domain Assembly
  readonly RBGC_SA_EMAIL_DOMAIN="iam.gserviceaccount.com"

  # API Version Paths
  readonly RBGC_IAM_V1="/v1"
  readonly RBGC_CRM_V1="/v1"
  readonly RBGC_CRM_V3="/v3"
  readonly RBGC_SERVICEUSAGE_V1="/v1"
  readonly RBGC_SERVICEUSAGE_V1BETA1="/v1beta1"
  readonly RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1="/v1"
  readonly RBGC_CLOUDBUILD_V1="/v1"
  readonly RBGC_CLOUDBILLING_V1="/v1"

  count=$(grep -c "enshrines\|hallmarks\|reliquaries" "$f" 2>/dev/null)
  echo "$count  $f"
done | sort -rn

content.subject: Discover code literals (bash + rust)
content.description: Locate every `enshrines/`, `hallmarks/`, `reliquaries/` literal in bash and rust source; identify call sites composing GAR locators; identify the kindle/tinder module candidate.

content.subject: Discover populated rbrv.env and other artifacts
content.description: Find the populated `enshrines/...` ANCHOR field in `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/rbrv.env`; sweep for any other populated wire-format values across rbrv.env files.

content.subject: Compile master file list with classifications

1758:|`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/about:«HALLMARK»`

1783:Multiple conjures produce distinct hallmarks (unique realized timestamps) with independently-attested images.
1785:Hallmark-only lookup falls out of the layout: because every ark's packages live under `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/`, {rbtgo_ark_inspect} resolves an ark from a bare hallmark by navigating the subtree directly — no cross-vessel aggregator tag is needed.
1786:Look up `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»`, read `vouch_summary.json` to discover the originating vessel, then fetch `about` from the same subtree.
1793:the `image` package under the hallmark subtree (`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»`).
1814:the `about` package under the hallmark subtree (`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/about:«HALLMARK»`).
1840:the `vouch` package under the hallmark subtree (`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/vouch:«HALLMARK»`).
1895:**Abjure**: {rbtgo_ark_abjure} enumerates every package under `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/` and deletes each — `image`, `about`, `vouch`, `pouch`, `diags`, and (for conjure vessels) `attest`. Deleting the single `attest` package removes all its per-arch tags.
1904:the `pouch` package under the hallmark subtree (`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/pouch:«HALLMARK»`).
1917:the `diags` package under the hallmark subtree (`hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/diags:«HALLMARK»`).
1957:Hallmark subtree prefix — `«PREFIX»hallmarks/«HALLMARK»`.
2016:each pulled image is tagged as `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/attest:«HALLMARK»-«ARCH»`;
2040:  3. `buildx --push` — build all platforms, push consumer-facing `image` manifest list to GAR under `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»`
2041:  4. `per-platform-pullback` — `docker pull --platform «ARCH»` from the `image` package, `docker tag` to `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/attest:«HALLMARK»-«ARCH»`
2583:// rbtc_refresh_gcb_pins eliminated (₣Av) — RBRG regime removed, reliquaries replace pin refresh
2803:// RBRG variable definitions eliminated (₣Av) — reliquaries replace pin-based tool image management
2833:// RBRG regime eliminated (₣Av) — reliquaries replace pin-based tool image management
3184:The anchor itself is `«sanitized-origin»-«10-char-sha256»` (e.g., `python-3.11-slim-abc123def4`); the locator embeds it as both path segment and tag: `enshrines/«ANCHOR»:«ANCHOR»`.
3185:Enshrined images are stored as peer packages under the shared `enshrines/` namespace, not per-vessel — vessels sharing the same base image origin share one enshrined artifact.
3423:Format: `package-path:tag` where `package-path` is a relative path under the categorical root (e.g., `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/image`, `reliquaries/«STAMP»/syft`, `enshrines/«ANCHOR»`).
3427:Domain-blind: the package path embedded in the locator is sufficient to dispatch a delete or pull against any of the three categorical domains (hallmarks, reliquaries, enshrinements).
3443:Mints once at {rbtgo_depot_inscribe} time and serves both as the GAR path segment (`reliquaries/«STAMP»/...`) and the per-tool tag, so a single value identifies the namespace and pins every member.

83-
--

100-
--
110-
111:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/image:{INSCRIBE_TS}-multi`
112-|Intermediate `buildx --push` target (multi-platform only)
113-
114:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/image:{HALLMARK}-{SUFFIX}`
115-|Per-platform image tag on the image package (persistent, hallmark-tagged)
116-
117:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/image:{INSCRIBE_TS}-{SUFFIX}`
118-|SLSA alias tag (`images:` field, provenance generation trigger — ephemeral, overwritten on re-conjure)
119-
120:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/image:{HALLMARK}`
121-|Consumer-facing manifest list on the image package (reassembled by `imagetools create`, multi-platform only)
122-
123:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/about:{HALLMARK}`

125-

127-|Build-time diagnostics container on the diags package (conjure only — `buildkit_metadata.json`, `cache_before.json`, `cache_after.json`, `recipe.txt`). Pushed by conjure step 07, pulled by about step 01.

129:|`<prefix>hallmarks/{HALLMARK}/attest:{HALLMARK}-{SUFFIX}`

139-Contents are extracted by
--
157-|`buildkit_metadata.json`
158:|BuildKit resolved base image references and build parameters (conjure only, forwarded from the `hallmarks/<hallmark>/diags` package).
159-
160-|`cache_before.json`
161:|Docker daemon image state before the build (conjure only, forwarded from the `hallmarks/<hallmark>/diags` package).
162-
163-|`cache_after.json`
164:|Docker daemon image state after all pushes (conjure only, forwarded from the `hallmarks/<hallmark>/diags` package).
165-
--
167-
168:NOTE: The three enrichment files (`buildkit_metadata.json`, `cache_before.json`, `cache_after.json`) are present only for conjure hallmarks.
169:They are forwarded from the `hallmarks/<hallmark>/diags` registry package by the about pipeline's step 1.
170-For bind and graft, these files are absent and the about artifact contains only `build_info.json`, `sbom.json`, and optionally `recipe.txt`.
--
188-**Dependency mirroring is universal.**
189:All GCB step images come from reliquaries (inscribed tool images in GAR).
190-All base images come from anchors (enshrined base images in GAR).

1	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
2	# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
3	#
4	# Recipe Bottle base compose file — static infrastructure
5	#
6	# Invoked via:
7	#   docker compose --env-file .rbk/rbrr.env --env-file .rbk/{moniker}/rbrn.env \
8	#     -f .rbk/rbob_compose.yml [-f .rbk/{moniker}/compose.yml] -p {moniker} up -d
9	#
10	# Env-file variables serve dual purpose:
11	#   1. YAML interpolation (${VAR} in this file — image refs, subnet, container names)
12	#   2. Container environment forwarding (bare names in environment: sections)
13	#
14	# Network topology:
15	#   sentry: default (internet via bridge) + enclave (eth0=default priority:1000, eth1=enclave priority:500)
16	#   pentacle: enclave only (static IP, routes through sentry)
17	#   bottle: shares pentacle network namespace (network_mode: service:pentacle)
18	#
19	# Health chain: sentry healthy → pentacle starts → pentacle healthy → bottle starts (sessile profile)
20	
21	services:
22	  sentry:
23	    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
24	    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-sentry
25	    privileged: true
26	    networks:
27	      default:
28	        priority: 1000
29	      enclave:
30	        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}
31	        priority: 500
32	    ports:
33	      - "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"
34	    environment:
35	      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP
36	      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK
37	      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
38	      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP
39	      - RBRR_DNS_SERVER
40	      - RBRN_ENTRY_MODE
41	      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION
42	      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE
43	      - RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE
44	      - RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN
45	      - RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE
46	      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS
47	      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS
48	      - RBJE_PROBE
49	    healthcheck:
50	      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjh_healthy"]
51	      interval: 2s
52	      timeout: 5s
53	      retries: 15
54	      start_period: 5s
55	
56	  pentacle:
57	    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
58	    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-pentacle
59	    privileged: true
60	    networks:
61	      enclave:
62	        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}
63	    environment:
64	      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
65	    command: ["/opt/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh"]
66	    depends_on:
67	      sentry:
68	        condition: service_healthy
69	    healthcheck:
70	      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjph_healthy"]
71	      interval: 2s
72	      timeout: 5s
73	      retries: 15
74	      start_period: 5s
75	
76	  bottle:
77	    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}
78	    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-bottle
79	    network_mode: "service:pentacle"
80	    security_opt:
81	      - label:disable
82	    cap_drop:
83	      - NET_RAW
84	    environment:
85	      - RBOB_HOST_UID
86	      - RBOB_HOST_GID
87	      - RBRV_USER
88	    depends_on:
89	      pentacle:
90	        condition: service_healthy

# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
#
# Recipe Bottle base compose file — static infrastructure
#
# Invoked via:
#   docker compose --env-file .rbk/rbrr.env --env-file .rbk/{moniker}/rbrn.env \
#     -f .rbk/rbob_compose.yml [-f .rbk/{moniker}/compose.yml] -p {moniker} up -d
#
# Env-file variables serve dual purpose:
#   1. YAML interpolation (${VAR} in this file — image refs, subnet, container names)
#   2. Container environment forwarding (bare names in environment: sections)
#
# Network topology:
#   sentry: default (internet via bridge) + enclave (eth0=default priority:1000, eth1=enclave priority:500)
#   pentacle: enclave only (static IP, routes through sentry)
#   bottle: shares pentacle network namespace (network_mode: service:pentacle)
#
# Health chain: sentry healthy → pentacle starts → pentacle healthy → bottle starts (sessile profile)

services:
  sentry:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-sentry
    privileged: true
    networks:
      default:
        priority: 1000
      enclave:
        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}
        priority: 500
    ports:
      - "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"
    environment:
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP
      - RBRR_DNS_SERVER
      - RBRN_ENTRY_MODE
      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION
      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS
      - RBJE_PROBE
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjh_healthy"]
      interval: 2s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 15
      start_period: 5s

  pentacle:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-pentacle
    privileged: true
    networks:
      enclave:
        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}
    environment:
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
    command: ["/opt/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh"]
    depends_on:
      sentry:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjph_healthy"]
      interval: 2s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 15
      start_period: 5s

  bottle:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-bottle
    network_mode: "service:pentacle"
    security_opt:
      - label:disable
    cap_drop:
      - NET_RAW
    environment:
      - RBOB_HOST_UID
      - RBOB_HOST_GID
      - RBRV_USER
    depends_on:
      pentacle:
        condition: service_healthy

Each anchor is its own package under the namespace — the path segment carries the anchor identity, and the tag redundantly restates it so divergence is a visible bug. The anchor is vessel-independent — multiple vessels sharing the same base image origin share one enshrined package.

1	= Recipe Bottle Specification
2	:toc:
3	:toc-title: Table of Contents
4	:sectnums:
5	:icons: font
6	:index:
7	
8	// Begin Mapping Section
9	// tag::mapping-section[]
10	
11	// Category declarations:
12	// rbtr_ prefix: RB Term Role (Recipe Bottle role identities)
13	// rbtgi_ prefix: RB Term Google Instance (Recipe Bottle specific instances)
14	// rbtgo_ prefix: RB Google Operation (Recipe Bottle workflows)
15	// gcp_ prefix: Google Cloud Platform (core infrastructure concepts)
16	// gar_ prefix: Google Artifact Registry (generic service)
17	// gcs_ prefix: Google Cloud Storage (generic service)
18	// gcb_ prefix: Google Cloud Build (generic service)
19	// giam_ prefix: Google Cloud Identity and Access Management
20	// rbtc_ prefix: RB Term Colophon (tabtarget entry points)
21	// rbrr_ prefix: Repository Regime configuration
22	
23	// Local Architecture Terms (bottle deployment architecture):
24	// at_ prefix: Architectural Term (core system components)
25	// st_ prefix: Support Term (infrastructure and technical concepts)
26	
27	// Variant patterns:
28	//   _s: plural form
29	//   _p: possessive form
30	//   _ed: past tense (verbs)
31	//   _ing: progressive form (verbs)
32	
33	// RB Role Infrastructure
34	:rbtr_role:                  <<rbtr_role,RB Role>>
35	:rbtr_role_s:                <<rbtr_role,RB Roles>>
36	
37	// Service Account Hierarchy
38	:rbtr_consumer:              <<rbtr_consumer,Consumer Role>>
39	:rbtr_consumer_p:            <<rbtr_consumer,Consumer Role's>>
40	:rbtr_consumer_s:            <<rbtr_consumer,Consumer Roles>>
41	:rbtr_director:              <<rbtr_director,Director Role>>
42	:rbtr_director_s:            <<rbtr_director,Director Roles>>
43	:rbtr_governor:              <<rbtr_governor,Governor Role>>
44	:rbtr_marshal:               <<rbtr_marshal,Marshal Role>>
45	:rbtr_mason:                 <<rbtr_mason,Mason Role>>
46	:at_operator:                <<at_operator,Operator>>
47	:at_operator_p:              <<at_operator,Operator's>>
48	:at_operator_s:              <<at_operator,Operators>>
49	:rbtr_payor:                 <<rbtr_payor,Payor Role>>
50	:rbtr_payor_p:               <<rbtr_payor,Payor Role's>>
51	:rbtr_retriever:             <<rbtr_retriever,Retriever Role>>
52	:rbtr_retriever_s:           <<rbtr_retriever,Retriever Roles>>
53	
54	// Recipe Bottle Term Google Instances
55	:rbtgi_build_bucket:         <<rbtgi_build_bucket,RB Build Bucket>>
56	:rbtgi_build_bucket_s:       <<rbtgi_build_bucket,RB Build Buckets>>
57	:rbtgi_repository:           <<rbtgi_repository,RB Container Repository>>
58	:rbtgi_repository_s:         <<rbtgi_repository,RB Container Repositories>>
59	:rbtgi_depot:                <<rbtgi_depot,RB Depot>>
60	:rbtgi_depot_project:        <<rbtgi_depot_project,RB Depot Project>>
61	:rbtgi_depot_project_s:      <<rbtgi_depot_project,RB Depot Projects>>
62	:rbtgi_image:                <<rbtgi_image,RB Image>>
63	:rbtgi_image_s:              <<rbtgi_image,RB Images>>

1136:Build context delivered via {rbtga_ark_pouch} (FROM SCRATCH OCI image in GAR).
1149:mirrors upstream tool images into a datestamped GAR namespace
1160:validates the stamp against GAR and the vessel against its enrollment, then rewrites `RBRV_RELIQUARY` in the vessel's `rbrv.env`
1207:Lists identifiers under one of three GAR domains (hallmarks, reliquaries, enshrinements) in
1253:{gar_service}
1262:submits a Cloud Build job that copies each upstream image by tag to GAR with a human-readable anchor tag, and writes the anchor back to the vessel regime.
1650:=== {gar_service} Definitions
1656:{gar_service}
1732:* `g` — graft (local build pushed to GAR): `g«YYMMDDHHMMSS»-r«YYMMDDHHMMSS»`
1735:For conjure, bind, and graft: `«MODE»YYMMDDHHMMSS-rYYMMDDHHMMSS` where the `r` prefix means "realized" — the moment the image materialized in GAR.
1740:T2 (r-prefixed): when the image landed in GAR.
1745:For kludge, the hallmark uses a git-context suffix instead of a realized timestamp, since no GAR push occurs.
1772:|Build context (FROM SCRATCH OCI image in GAR)
1794:Contains the executable container layers, regardless of how the image arrived in GAR.
1851:* *Bind*: Digest-pin comparison — verifies the image digest in GAR matches the pinned upstream reference. No SLSA provenance exists for upstream images.
1867:Vouch uses the RunBuild API (`builds.create`) rather than trigger dispatch — the hallmark is known only after the image arrives in GAR, so a pre-registered trigger cannot provide it.
1905:A FROM SCRATCH OCI image in GAR containing the Dockerfile, step scripts, and regime-derived configuration needed to reproduce the build.
1908:The pouch is assembled on the host by the ordain pipeline and pushed to GAR before the Cloud Build job is submitted.
1952:GAR registry root path.
1997:Cloud Build pre-populates Docker credentials for all GAR regions, so explicit docker-login steps are unnecessary.
2014:`buildx --push` builds all platforms and pushes the consumer-facing `image` manifest list directly to GAR;
2020:Cloud Build has no awareness of the `buildx --push` to GAR — the `images:` field is CB's only mechanism for pushing and attesting images.
2040:  3. `buildx --push` — build all platforms, push consumer-facing `image` manifest list to GAR under `hallmarks/«HALLMARK»/image:«HALLMARK»`
2042:  5. `push-per-platform` — push each per-arch `attest` tag to GAR
2249:Recipe Bottle's remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via {gcb_service} and {gar_service}.
2658:{gar_service}
2834:// Tool images now sourced from datestamped reliquary namespace in GAR at conjure time
3102:Selects grafting configuration: vessel accepts a locally-built image pushed to GAR.
3179:naming a GAR-mirrored base image for
3186:When present, conjure resolves the base image from GAR via this locator (parsing path and tag, prepending {rbrr_cloud_prefix} at use-site per the {rbst_locator} convention).
3440:Datestamp identifying a reliquary — the GAR namespace holding a coherent set of mirrored tool images for builder use.
3443:Mints once at {rbtgo_depot_inscribe} time and serves both as the GAR path segment (`reliquaries/«STAMP»/...`) and the per-tool tag, so a single value identifies the namespace and pins every member.

content.content: Updated task #5 status

content.text:
Discovery complete. Here is the master list.

---

## Critical finding: centralization mechanism already exists

The bash centralization the docket envisions is **already in place**:

- `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh:133-135` — kindle constants `RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_{HALLMARKS,RELIQUARIES,ENSHRINES}` (currently `="hallmarks"`, etc.)
- `Tools/rbk/rbgl_GarLayout.sh:48-50` — derived roots `RBGL_{HALLMARKS,RELIQUARIES,ENSHRINES}_ROOT` composed from `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` + the categories
- All bash callers compose locators via `${RBGL_*_ROOT}` rather than literals (with the YAML exception below)

So this pace is mostly: **change the literal values** + **propagate to spec corpus** + **migrate one rbrv.env line** + **fix a few holdouts**.

## BCG kindle/tinder placement

Per BCG line 131, **tinder constants** are pure-string-literal, source-time, lowercase-name (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`). Per line 363+, the choice is purely on runtime-dependency: pure literals can be either, but the surrounding convention in `rbgc_Constants.sh` puts ALL constants — even pure literals like `RBGC_ADMIN_ROLE="rbw-admin"` — inside the kindle function for `readonly` enforcement. The new tokens belong **alongside the existing trio at `rbgc_Constants.sh:133-135`** as kindle constants. They keep the SCREAMING `RBGC_*` shape.

## Naming question — fork

The docket's example name `RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"` would imply minting a new `RBI` module prefix, which the docket itself flags as **out of scope** ("Code-identifier prefix mintings ... were considered and rejected — wire-format only"). The clean reading: keep the constants in `rbgc_Constants.sh` under the existing `RBGC_*` prefix.

Three name options — pick one:

| Option | Names | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Value-only change** | `RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS="rbi_hm"` (etc.) | Most load-bearing-only — the existing names ARE descriptive; only values change |
| **B. Rename to "namespace"** | `RBGC_GAR_NAMESPACE_HALLMARKS="rbi_hm"` | Aligns vocabulary with docket's "namespace path tokens"; touches all bash/rust call sites symbolically |
| **C. Docket-literal** | `RBI_HALLMARKS_GAR_NAMESPACE="rbi_hm"` | Mints new prefix — violates docket's own out-of-scope rule |

**My recommendation: Option A.** Minimum surface, satisfies "names ought to be descriptive" (they already are), and the load-bearing change is the value. If you want the rename, B is reasonable but expands the diff.

---

## Master file list

### 1. Bash kindle constants — source of truth
- `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh:133-135` — change the three values; possibly rename per option above

### 2. Rust constant duplicate (must change to match)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2362` — `const RBTDRC_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS: &str = "hallmarks"` — Rust-side duplicate, value-change required

### 3. Rust test assertion using literal value
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs:1140` — assertion text `"hallmarks_root=acme-hallmarks"` becomes `"hallmarks_root=acme-rbi_hm"`

### 4. YAML compose file — bypasses the bash constant
- `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml:23, 57, 77` — three lines containing literal `${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}hallmarks/`. **Decision needed:** simple value change to `${RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX}rbi_hm/`, or introduce a derived env-var `${RBDC_GAR_HALLMARKS_NAMESPACE}` that gets exported via `--env-file`. The docket's "all bash/rust call sites" wording explicitly excludes YAML, so the simple literal change is in-bounds.

### 5. Populated wire-format value (only one repo-wide)
- `rbev-vessels/rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge/rbrv.env:41` — `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=enshrines/...` → `rbi_es/...`

### 6. Handbook example display
- `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhoda_director_airgap.sh:167` — example value shown to operator: `RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=enshrines/...` → `rbi_es/...`

### 7. Bash doc-string example values (operator-facing help text)
- `Tools/rbk/rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh:329, 399, 415, 732` — `e.g. hallmarks/H/image:H` and `(e.g. hallmarks/H/..., reliquaries/r..., enshrines/eb-...)` in doc-brief/doc-param strings shown by `rbw-i*` tabtargets

### 8. Spec corpus — RBS0 mapping section (NEW attribute references)
Add three plain-string attribute references near `gar_*` block (RBS0 line 178 area). Proposed:

```adoc
// Wire-format namespace path tokens (plain string substitutions, not linked-term quoins)
// Consumed by spec prose with `{attr}/` syntax to keep wire values centralized.
:gar_hallmarks_namespace:    rbi_hm
:gar_reliquaries_namespace:  rbi_rq
:gar_enshrines_namespace:    rbi_es
```

### 9. Spec corpus — wire-format use sites (sweep `literal/` → `{attr}/`)

| File | Wire-format lines | Approx count |
|---|---|---|
| `RBS0-SpecTop.adoc` | 1697, 1754, 1758, 1762, 1766, 1770, 1774, 1785, 1786, 1793, 1814, 1840, 1895, 1904, 1917, 1957, 2016, 2040, 2041, 3184, 3185, 3423, 3443 | ~23 |
| `RBSAB-ark_about.adoc` | 26, 46, 57, 59, 61, 100, 107 | 7 |
| `RBSAC-ark_conjure.adoc` | (3 lines) | 3 |
| `RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc` | 51, 62 (×2), 63 | 4 |
| `RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc` | 82, 99, 111, 114, 117, 120, 123, 126, 129, 138, 158, 161, 164, 169 (+others) | ~17 |
| `RBSCL-consecration_tally.adoc` | (8 lines) | 8 |
| `RBSAV-ark_vouch.adoc` | (7 lines) | 7 |
| `RBSAP-ark_plumb.adoc` | (6 lines) | 6 |
| `RBSDV-director_vouch.adoc` | (4 lines) | 4 |
| `RBSAK-ark_kludge.adoc` | (4 lines) | 4 |
| `RBSAS-ark_summon.adoc` | (3 lines) | 3 |
| `RBSAG-ark_graft.adoc` | (3 lines) | 3 |
| `RBSIA-image_audit.adoc` | 16, 17, 18, 40, 92, 144 (+others) | ~6 wire of 13 |
| `RBSIR-image_rekon.adoc` | (≤2 lines) | ≤2 |
